chemistry.reaction_name: Blue-light-mediated decarboxylative C-H activation of isoquinoline
chemistry.scope_summary: Minisci-type C-H functionalization of heteroarenes like isoquinoline with dimethoxyphenyl acetic acid under blue light irradiation to form papaverine analogues

optimization_table.key_findings: 465 nm LED optimal for trifluoromethylation; reaction time reduced from 30 min batch to 7 min flow with improved yield

figures.flow_path: multi-level circular flow path with batch processing capability
figures.key_information: PhotoFlexys: A Multi-Purpose Reactor Platform to Simplify Process Intensification and Mechanistic Studies in Photochemistry - shows PAT (Process Analytical Technology) integration with batch and flow capabilities

figures.flow_path: flow splitter distributes to reaction chamber
figures.reactor_geometry: cylindrical reaction vessel with modular top
figures.light_source_position: LED arrays positioned in functional top above reaction chamber
figures.key_information: Modular photoreactor system with interchangeable functional tops for batch, multi-batch, flow, and in-situ analytics configurations. Features LED illumination, temperature control via hot plate and internal cooling, and flow distribution system.
figures.caption: Figure 1.  A) Exploded view of the designed PhotoFlexys reactor platform with interchangeable LED arrays and operation modes. B) Cutaway view of the internal irradiation chamber and representation of the reactor's internal cooling architecture. C) Workflow for adapting specific operation modes from a standardized basic top.

Figure 2.  A) Evolution of the reactor's internal temperature with and without cooling and its effect on the irradiance. B) Comparison of actinometry and radiometric measurements for evaluation of the available photon flux

Figure 3.  Spectroradiometric irradiance mapping on a vertical slice of the photoreactor. Experimental results for the LED arrays equipped with either the 465 nm emitters (A, emission angle 80 °) or the 450 nm emitter (B, emission angle 130 °). | Figure 4.  Blue-light mediated trifluoromethylation of mesitylene 3. A) Kinetic profiles of the reaction under batch and continuous flow conditions as well as a comparison to a commercially available setup. B) Reactor performance in a parallel multi-batch mode. Yields were determined by 19F-NMR spectroscopy.

figures.trend: yield increases rapidly initially then plateaus
figures.caption: Figure 3.  Spectroradiometric irradiance mapping on a vertical slice of the photoreactor. Experimental results for the LED arrays equipped with either the 465 nm emitters (A, emission angle 80 °) or the 450 nm emitter (B, emission angle 130 °). | Figure 4.  Blue-light mediated trifluoromethylation of mesitylene 3. A) Kinetic profiles of the reaction under batch and continuous flow conditions as well as a comparison to a commercially available setup. B) Reactor performance in a parallel multi-batch mode. Yields were determined by 19F-NMR spectroscopy.

figures.trend: Both batch and flow reactions show increasing yield over time, with flow chemistry reaching higher yields faster than batch
figures.caption: Figure 5.  Photooxidation of α-terpinene performed under batch and continuous flow conditions.

figures.x_axis: Various parameters (Time, Oxygen pressure, Photon fluence rate, RB concentration, Time on stream)
figures.trend: Multiple trends shown across different parameters
figures.caption: Figure 6.  Blue-light-mediated decarboxylative C–H activation of isoquinoline. A) Reaction monitoring by in-situ mIR-spectroscopy. B) Dependence of the reaction kinetics on the applied irradiation wavelength. The catalyst absorption is presented in grey. C) Comparison of the reaction yield in a batch and continuous flow run.Yields were determined by 1H-NMR spectroscopy. | Figure 7.  Photooxygenation of 2-furoic acid (2-FA). A) Kinetic profile of the reaction under batch and continuous flow conditions. B–D) Obtained reaction kinetics from in-situ Raman measurements as a function of different process parameters. E) Long-term run under continuous flow conditions.

figures.trend: Both batch and flow reactions show rapid initial increase in yield, then plateau, with flow reaching slightly higher yield faster
figures.caption: Figure 6.  Blue-light-mediated decarboxylative C–H activation of isoquinoline. A) Reaction monitoring by in-situ mIR-spectroscopy. B) Dependence of the reaction kinetics on the applied irradiation wavelength. The catalyst absorption is presented in grey. C) Comparison of the reaction yield in a batch and continuous flow run.Yields were determined by 1H-NMR spectroscopy. | Figure 7.  Photooxygenation of 2-furoic acid (2-FA). A) Kinetic profile of the reaction under batch and continuous flow conditions. B–D) Obtained reaction kinetics from in-situ Raman measurements as a function of different process parameters. E) Long-term run under continuous flow conditions.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Slower reaction kinetics and lower yields observed in batch processing
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Enhanced mass transfer and light penetration leading to faster reaction rates and improved yields
translation_logic.what_changed: Residence time reduced from 30 min to 7 min, concentration increased from 0.1 M to 0.25 M
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: LED wavelength (465 nm), solvent (MeCN), oxygen atmosphere, photocatalyst system
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Continuous flow processing reduces inventory and provides better temperature control

figure_descriptions: Modular reactor design with interchangeable LED arrays
Kinetic comparison plots for batch vs flow conditions
Optimization plots for various photochemical reactions

conflicts: Multiple photochemical reactions presented - focused on decarboxylative C-H activation as primary example
Some yield values vary between different optimization plots but are consistent within each reaction type
notes: Paper presents a multi-purpose photoreactor platform (PhotoFlexys) with multiple benchmark reactions. Extraction focused on the blue-light-mediated decarboxylative C-H activation of isoquinoline as the primary example. The platform enables both batch and flow processing with inline analytics (ATR-UV/Vis, ATR-mIR, Raman spectroscopy). Time reduction factor calculated from 30 min batch to 7 min flow residence time. Yield improvement from 60% to 75% represents a 25% relative increase.
